title: AES, Block Ciphers, and Primitive Boundaries · Runtime Contract: Crypto Go Known Answer Test
summary: A developer calls AES.Encrypt directly on repeated application records, invents padding, and assumes a strong block cipher alone protects a message. Predict the asset, adversary, security property, trust boundary, and falsifying evidence before opening the governing standard. Planned artifact: Add and verify the known answer test increment in AES primitive boundary and known-answer harness. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evide…
objectives: Use authoritative known-answer vectors to verify byte order, exact inputs, exact outputs, and implementation wiring without treating vectors as a security proof.
Validate key and block lengths before using low-level AES APIs and convert panics or errors into bounded caller behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

theory case — A theory cryptographic engineering team handles case 5092 during a credential migration window. Production entropy and keys, secret custody, native timing and side channels, networks, TLS, HSM/KMS, certificate systems, FIPS or compliance state, load, faults, restore, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. content.code:
package main

import (
	"crypto/aes"
	"encoding/hex"
	"fmt"
)

func main() {
	key := make([]byte, 16)
	key[0] = 2
	plaintext := []byte("0123456789ABCDEF")
	block, err := aes.NewCipher(key)
	if err != nil { panic(err) }
	ciphertext := make([]byte, aes.BlockSize)
	recovered := make([]byte, aes.BlockSize)
	block.Encrypt(ciphertext, plaintext)
	block.Decrypt(recovered, ciphertext)
	fmt.Printf("block=%d ciphertext=%s recovered=%t message_mode=false\n", aes.BlockSize, hex.EncodeToString(ciphertext), string(recovered) == string(plaintext))
}
